items.title: Anaphylaxis: initial management
items.contentHtml:
Anaphylaxis: first-line intramuscular adrenaline

  • Intramuscular adrenaline is the first-line drug in anaphylaxis; dose: 0.01 mg/kg (maximum 0.5 mg per dose) in the anterolateral aspect of the thigh.
  • The intramuscular route offers faster and safer absorption than the subcutaneous or intravenous route; Delay in its administration is associated with greater mortality.
  • Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuvants, they never replace adrenaline; glucagon is used if the patient takes beta blockers and does not respond to adrenaline.
  • Diagnostic criteria: acute onset (minutes to hours) with compromise of the airway, breathing or circulation, plus skin/mucosal involvement in most cases.

items.title: Hereditary angioedema
items.contentHtml:
Hereditary angioedema: C1 inhibitor deficiency

  • The hereditary angioedema is due to a quantitative (type I, 85%) or functional (type II, 15%) deficiency of the C1 esterase inhibitor, with autosomal dominant inheritance.
  • Clinically: recurrent episodes of nonpruritic subcutaneous and submucosal edema, without urticaria, affecting extremities, abdomen (colic pain) and airway (risk of suffocation).
  • Diagnosis: low levels of C4 and C1 esterase inhibitor (antigenic and functional); C4 complement is the best screening.
  • Acute treatment: icatibant (bradykinin receptor antagonist) or C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate; prophylaxis with danazol (attenuated androgen).

items.foco: Skin tests: prick test indication and reading
items.title: Prick test: technique and interpretation
items.contentHtml:
Skin tests: prick test indication and reading

  • The prick test is indicated to detect specific IgE sensitization to inhalant allergens (pollens, mites, epithelia) and some foods; It is performed on the forearm or back.
  • Technique: drop of allergenic extract on the skin, punctured with a lancet through the drop; reading after 15-20 minutes.
  • Positive reading: papule ≥ 3 mm in diameter larger than the negative control (saline solution); positive control with histamine (10 mg/mL) must give a papule ≥ 3 mm to validate the test.
  • False negatives due to antihistamines (discontinue 3-7 days according to half-life), false positives due to dermographism.

items.title: Allergic rhinitis: ARIA classification
items.contentHtml:
Allergic rhinitis: intermittent/persistent classification

  • According to the ARIA classification (Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma), allergic rhinitis is divided into intermittent (symptoms <4 days/week or <4 consecutive weeks) and persistent (symptoms ≥4 days/week and ≥4 consecutive weeks).
  • In addition, it is classified by severity: mild (normal sleep, daily activities without impairment) and moderate-severe (at least one criterion of impairment of sleep, activities, work or school).
  • First line: intranasal corticosteroids; Second-generation oral antihistamines as an alternative in mild intermittent forms.
  • Trap: the seasonal/perennial classification is no longer the main one; The ARIA prioritizes duration and intensity.

items.foco: Chronic urticaria: first choice H1 antihistamines
items.title: Chronic urticaria: initial treatment
items.contentHtml:
Chronic urticaria: first-choice H1 antihistamines

  • Chronic urticaria (duration >6 weeks) has as its first line second generation H1 antihistamines (e.g. loratadine, cetirizine, fexofenadine) at standard doses.
  • If there is no response in 2-4 weeks, the dose is increased up to 4 times the dose. standard (according to EAACI/GA²LEN guidelines).
  • Underlying causes are ruled out: infections, drugs (NSAIDs), autoimmune diseases (thyroid); In the majority it is idiopathic.
  • Do not use chronic systemic corticosteroids; in refractory cases, consider omalizumab (anti-IgE antibody) as a third line.

items.title: Penicillin allergy: diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Penicillin allergy: hapten skin test

  • The hapten skin test for penicillin allergy includes the major determinant (penicilloyl-polylysine) and minor determinants (penicillin G, penicillin, peniloate).
  • Prick test is performed first; if negative, intradermorreaction with immediate reading after 20 minutes; overall sensitivity of 70-90% for immediate IgE-mediated reactions.
  • Negative skin test does not exclude late allergy (maculopapular rashes); for non-immediate reactions, patch testing or oral challenge is used.
  • Trap: amoxicillin alone is not sufficient as a hapten; It must include the major and minor determiners.

items.foco: Allergic asthma: elevated IgE and sputum eosinophilia
items.title: Allergic asthma: biomarkers
items.contentHtml:
Allergic asthma: elevated IgE and sputum eosinophilia

  • Allergic asthma is characterized by elevated total serum IgE and sputum eosinophilia (≥3% eosinophils in induced sample), along with positive skin tests to aeroallergens.
  • Sputum eosinophilia is the standard for phenotype. Th2; a count >2% predicts a good response to inhaled corticosteroids and anti-IL5 biologics.
  • Total IgE >100 IU/mL (or >normal value according to age) supports the diagnosis, but is not specific; allergen-specific IgE (RAST) confirms sensitization.
  • Trap: non-allergic (intrinsic) asthma presents with normal IgE and low sputum eosinophilia; Treatment differs.

items.foco: Desensitization: indications in allergy to Hymenoptera
items.title: Desensitization in Hymenoptera
items.contentHtml:
Desensitization: indications in hymenoptera allergy

  • Immunotherapy with hymenoptera venom (bee, wasp) is indicated in patients with a history of severe systemic reaction (Mueller grade III-IV) and positive tests (prick or specific IgE).
  • Relative contraindications: active autoimmune diseases, use of beta blockers, uncontrolled asthma; in children with only mild skin reactions it is not routinely recommended.
  • Efficacy is >95% for wasp venom and 80-90% for bee venom; The usual duration is 3-5 years, after which it is considered to suspend if tolerance is maintained.
  • Scheme: accumulation phase (increasing weekly doses) and maintenance phase (dose every 4-6 weeks).

items.foco: Food allergy: milk, egg, peanut most common
items.title: Food allergy: prevalence
items.contentHtml:
Food allergy: milk, egg, peanut most common

  • In children, the most common allergens are cow's milk, egg and peanut; In adults, nuts, shellfish and fish predominate.
  • Milk and egg allergy usually resolves in childhood (60-80% at 5-10 years), while peanut allergy persists in most cases.
  • Clinical manifestations: urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis, oral allergy syndrome (lip itching/edema with fresh fruits due to cross-reactivity with pollens).
  • Diagnosis: clinical history + prick test/specific IgE; the gold standard is the oral challenge test.

items.foco: Atopic dermatitis: Hanifin and Rajka criteria
items.title: Atopic dermatitis: diagnostic criteria
items.contentHtml:
Atopic dermatitis: Hanifin and Rajka criteria

  • The Hanifin and Rajka criteria require at least 3 major and 3 minor criteria for the diagnosis of atopic dermatitis.
  • Major criteria: pruritus, typical morphology and distribution (flexural in adults, facial and extensor in infants), chronic or relapsing dermatitis, personal/family history of atopy (asthma, allergic rhinitis, atopic dermatitis).
  • Minor criteria include: xerosis, ichthyosis, palmar hyperlinearity, keratosis pilaris, pityriasis alba, Dennie-Morgan fold, keratoconus, anterior subcapsular cataract, among others.
  • Trap: in infants the distribution is on the cheeks and extensor surfaces; Do not use isolated major criteria without a history of atopy.

items.foco: Oral challenge test: gold standard in food allergy
items.title: Oral provocation in food allergy
items.contentHtml:
Oral provocation test: gold standard in food allergy

  • The oral provocation test is the gold standard to confirm or rule out food allergy; It is carried out in a controlled environment with medical supervision.
  • Types: open (patient and doctor know the food), single-blind (patient does not know) or double-blind placebo-controlled (DBCPC) which is the most rigorous for research.
  • Increasing doses of the food are administered every 15-30 minutes until the defined cumulative dose is reached or until objective symptoms appear.
  • Contraindications: recent anaphylaxis (<4 weeks), uncontrolled asthma, use of beta blockers; resuscitation equipment and intramuscular adrenaline must be available.

items.foco: Latex allergy: association with spina bifida and health personnel
items.title: Latex allergy: risk groups
items.contentHtml:
Latex allergy: association with spina bifida and health personnel

  • Latex allergy is a type I (IgE) hypersensitivity reaction to natural rubber proteins; risk groups: health personnel (repeated exposure to gloves) and patients with spina bifida (exposure from early surgeries).
  • In spina bifida, the prevalence reaches 40-65% due to multiple surgical interventions and catheterizations; in health personnel, 5-15%.
  • Clinical: contact urticaria, angioedema, rhinitis, occupational asthma, intraoperative anaphylaxis; cross allergy with banana, avocado, kiwi (latex-fruit syndrome).
  • Management: avoid exposure, use synthetic gloves (nitrile, neoprene), wear alert bracelet; in the operating room, latex-free environment for sensitized patients.
